content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

June 1, 1977


James A. McKelvey
Chief Assistant
Office of the City Attorney
City Hall
2326 Fresno Street
Fresno, CA 93721

Re: Opinion Request No. 77-016

Dear Mr. McKelvey:

Thank you for your letter of May 9, 1977, in which you
request an opinion regarding filing requirements under Chapter 4
of the Political Reform Act. Specifically, you asked if Government Code Section 84200 requires a city council candidate who is
defeated in a general municipal election held on March 8 to file
overlapping campaign statements if he again seeks election to
the city council in a special municipal election held on May 31.
Since your letter raises no substantial questions involving an
interpretation of the Act, no formal opinion will be issued.
However, I hope the following informal comments will be helpful.

Government Code Section 84200 requires candidates and
committees supporting or opposing candidates to file campaign
statements not later than 40 days and 12 days prior to the election and not later than 5 days after the election. In addition,
Section 84210 requires that each campaign statement shall contain the total amount of contributions received and expenditures
made during the period covered by the campaign statement.

Consequently, a candidate who runs for office in a
March 8 election as well as a May 31 election should file campaign
statements as follows:

35

James P. McKelvey
Page two
June 1, 1977


Type of                   Deadline         Period Covered
Campaign Statement         Date

1st Pre-March 8           January 27       From the closing date of
Election                                   the most recent campaign
                                          statement of, if a cam-
                                          paign statement has never
                                          been filed, from the date
                                          of the first reportable
                                          transaction during the
                                          current calendar year
                                          through January 24.

2nd Pre-March 8           February 24      January 25 - February 21
Election

1st Pre-May 31            April 21         February 22 - April 18
Election

Post-March 8              May 12           April 19 - May 5
Election

2nd Pre-May 31            May 19           May 6 - May 16
Election

Post-May 31               August 4         May 17 - July 23
Election


        The above schedule is applicable for candidates who
run for office in both elections. A candidate who runs for of-
fice only in the second election would file only the statements
for that election and the period covered for the first statement
would be from the closing date of the candidate's most recent
campaign statement or, if the candidate had never filed a state-
ment, from the date of the first reportable transaction during
the current calendar year.

        When candidates and committees supporting or opposing
candidates file according to the above schedule, they should keep
during two points in mind. First, all receipts and expenditures which
occurred during a reporting period must be reported on the cam-
paign statement which pertains to that period regardless of the
fact that the receipts or expenditures may have been received or
made in connection with different elections. See Government Code
Section 84210. Second, the obligation to cumulate contributions
and expenditures over more than one reporting period terminates
upon the filing of a postelection statement. See Government Code
Sections 84210 and 82018.

James A. McKelvey
Page three
June 1, 1977


In your letter you also ask if the answer to your
first question would be different respecting if a candidate who
completes his first campaign in a solvent condition with all
his liabilities paid as opposed to a candidate who completes
his first campaign with a deficit which he hopes to retire
from the proceeds of his second campaign.

Government Code Section 84205 provides, in part,
that "any campaign statement required to be filed after an
election by Sections 84200, 84201 and 84203 may be filed
prior to the closing date if all liabilities of the filer
have been paid and no additional contributions or expendi-
tures are anticipated." Consequently, if a candidate satis-
fies the requirements of Section 84205, he or she may file a
postelection statement early and, if the statement is filed
prior to April 18 (closing date for the first preeelection
statement for the second election), the candidate's cumulative
totals for the first election end, and subsequent filings will
not include receipts and expenditures in connection with the
first election.

I hope the above advice is helpful. If you have any
additional questions, please contact John C. Greenwood of our
staff. If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion request,
you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:JCG:jo

CITY
OF FRESNO
CALIFORNIA

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY

WORLD CAPITAL OF AGRIBUSINESS

CITY HALL • 2376 FRESNO STREET • FRESNO, CALIFORNIA 93721
PHONE 488-1326 AREA CODE 209

May 9, 1977


Fair Political Practices Commission
State of California
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Request for Opinion

Your opinion is requested, pursuant to California Government
Code Section 83114, in answer to the following questions:

(1) Does Government Code Section 84200 require a City-
Council candidate who is defeated in a general municipal
election held on March 8 of any given year to file overlapping
campaign statements if he again seeks election to the City
Council in a special municipal election held on May 31 of
such year (i.e., must he file a statement 65 days following
the first election covering the period from February 21-
May 5 and also file a statement 40 days prior to the second
election covering the period April 18-May 16)?

(2) Does the answer to (1) differ for one candidate who
completed the first campaign in a solvent condition with all
of his liabilities paid, as opposed to another candidate who
wound up his first campaign with a deficit which he hopes to
retire from the proceeds of his second campaign?

Thank you for your assistance.


SPENCER THOMAS, JR.
City Attorney

[signature]
James A. McKelvey
Chief Assistant

JAM/zd
